claim: The best AI services markets have four unique traits: low trust (work already outsourced), low judgment at the task level (automatable steps), high intelligence threshold (hard work requiring models + humans), and regulation can be beneficial
actionable: When evaluating markets for AI services companies, screen for these four traits to ensure product-market fit and sustainable competitive advantage
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

claim: Domain fluency is a critical founder attribute for AI services companies; direct experience is best, but learned domain knowledge through research is acceptable if it builds credibility
actionable: Either build a founding team from within your target industry, or conduct deep research to become credible with skeptical buyers in regulated spaces
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

claim: Variance in service delivery is the existential problem for AI services companies; customers will fire you faster for inconsistency than for being slower or more expensive
actionable: Build quality control systems that minimize output variance early; track consistency metrics like you would track DAU in software
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

claim: The early demand trap: signing too many pilot customers before you've built scalable product will trap you in manual operations and kill product development
actionable: Cap your first pilot customers to a small handful (3-5 max); resist early traction signals that would prevent building scalable automation
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

claim: Price AI services on value and outcomes, not cost-plus or undercutting: cost-plus captures upside permanently and undercutting signals low quality
actionable: Use per-unit pricing (per claim, per loan) or outcome-based pricing; avoid cost-plus models and competitive undercutting strategies
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

claim: AI operating leverage hypothesis: traditional services firms cap at 30% margins; pure software/agents have more margin but smaller TAMs; the bet is AI services can reach 50%+ margins on 2-3x bigger markets
actionable: Model your P&L trajectory toward 50%+ gross margins as automation improves; this is the value prop that makes AI services compelling
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

claim: Buying an existing services business and adding AI rarely works; building is almost always better unless you need a regulatory moat (e.g., insurance licensing) fast
actionable: Avoid the temptation to acquire legacy service businesses to shortcut product-market fit; build your own unless you specifically need regulatory protection
source_title: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company
